turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We will hear arguments next in Washington v. Chrisman. Mr. Carpenter, I think you may begin whenever you are ready.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The petitioner in this matter, the state, feels there are three principal issues which the petitioner feels necessary to discuss in detail. The first issue is whether or not exigent circumstances existed in this case that would have excused the officer of first procuring a search warrant prior to his entry into the dorm room and seizure of the marihuana seeds. Secondly, whether or not it was proper for the law enforcement officer to accompany the arrestee, Mr. Overdahl, to the dorm room when he asked to be allowed to return to that premises to secure ID from the premises. And thirdly, if this Court should determine that the officer acted in violation of the Fourth Amendment on the first two questions, whether or not this is an appropriate case for the Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule to be applied to. In other words, whether or not the Court should look at the good faith of the officer in this particular case and apply a good faith exception to the exclusionary rule.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is the basic predicate of your case that if an arresting officer has probable cause to make an arrest and does so, he may keep the arrested person at least in sight if not in hand from that point on?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That is correct, Mr. Chief Justice. Our approach is that in order to protect both the officer and protect other members of society, protect the arrestee himself and protect the evidence from being destroyed, it is nonsensical to suggest that the officer should allow the arrestee out of his immediate control and presence. And in order to that, if he wants to be a reasonable, polite, courteous officer, wants to do what this officer tried to do, try to conduct himself in a low key, professional manner particularly on a college campus, he needed to afford the arrestee the opportunity to return to his premises, and that's basically what we're saying.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It was a return to the premises for the benefit of the arrested person.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That's correct, it was to expedite the release of the arrested person, Mr. Overdahl.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: From your point of view, from your position, the officer could have appropriately walked in the room and stayed within two or three feet of this man at all times. Is that your position?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Wait a minute. If a man is arrest out on the street, you could carry him across town and go into his room?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: No. I'm not saying that. Only if he requests to return to his premises.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the man didn't invite him, did he?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well no, that's not really true. The officer asked for ID and the individual indicated, Mr. Overdahl indicated that he did not have ID. He asked the officer if he could return to the premises. The officer indicated well, in light of your custodial status I can't let you return to the premises unless I accompany you, and Mr. Overdahl said okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what else could he do?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, he could have said no, that's not okay. Take me down to the station house and book me, handcuff me.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And gone to jail.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: And gone to jail. The officer was trying to allow him the opportunity to--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was his choice. It was either let the officer go with him or go to jail.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Carpenter, I gather the officer had wanted the ID, did he not, to find out his age, to use as evidence?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, that's part of the reason he wanted an ID, but you have to remember--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he wanted it for at least that much, didn't he, for that reason?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --Well, that's true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did he give Miranda warnings?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: No, he did not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Shouldn't he have?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: No, I don't believe so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why, if he was going to use this as evidence, wasn't he?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, there were three reasons.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He was going to use it as evidence, wasn't he? That's why he wanted it.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, that may have been a collateral reason. But the principal reason he needed it, anytime you arrest an individual you have to ID that person.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if we think he should have been given Miranda warnings and he didn't have them... you agree he didn't... wouldn't that taint everything after that?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well,--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well wouldn't it? Under our cases?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --Not necessarily, because--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under our cases, that he should have had them?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --Well, what would be Mr. Chrisman's standing to complain about Mr. Overdahl's violation of his Miranda rights? That would be my answer. Plus, the officer had a right to be out in that public hallway, regardless of whether or not he asked for his age. The dorm room and public hallway of Orton Hall was public area; it constitutes an unprotected area, he had a right to be there. And that's where he saw the principal observations before he went in under what we say was exigent circumstances.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Carpenter, before you leave the point, I'm still a little uncertain. Why was the ID important?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: You have to remember... and you can look at pages 7, 9 and 10 of the Joint Appendix and read through those pages, and you will see that the officer had three violations he was aware of. He was aware of a violation of being in possession of alcohol and in violation of a Washington statute, not being 21 years of age.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right, but he didn't need the ID to establish that because the young man had admitted he was 19.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: At the elevator before they ascended to the 11th floor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Once he admitted it, then why did he need the ID?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Because he has two other violations.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what were they?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: One was a violation of the Washington Administrative Code which prohibits from having alcohol on the campus--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he's on the campus; he didn't need an ID for that.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --And the third one would be he had an open container of alcohol, another criminal violation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He didn't ID for that either, did he?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, you need it... I would gently suggest that you need an ID anytime you arrest a person.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why? If I'm arrested out on the street somewhere, do I have to have an ID?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well sooner or later, when you get booked in you are going to have to identify yourself, aren't you? Are we going to book him in as a John Doe and hold--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He knew his name. Do you have to have an ID in writing in order to identify yourself?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --Well, in 1978 there were approximately 16,000 students on WSU campus.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You're not suggesting that this man didn't know his own name, are you?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: No, but I would suggest that it would be rather foolhardy for an officer to take his word as to what his name was. There are a lot of students on that campus. We see a lot of John Smith's.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But didn't he intend to take him to the station?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, no, he was trying to allow him to be released.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does the record show that?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: I believe it does.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The man is a man that admits he's under 21, with a half a gallon of liquor.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That's right, a half a gallon of gin.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the policeman is going to let him go?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, he would issue--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes or no.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --He would issue him a uniform citation--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is it your story that he was going to turn him loose?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --Sure, he would issue him a uniform citation for a minor violation and then he would have to appear in District Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought you said a minute ago he had a choice of taking him to the room or jail.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That's right, if you don't furnish some ID, you're going to jail.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now you say he wasn't going to take him to jail; he was going to give him a slip of paper.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: No, let me back up an explain.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I hope so. [Laughter]

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: His choices are that if I can't resolve this with some adequate ID so I can identify you and issue you a uniform citation so you can appear in seven days in Whitman County District Court to answer the criminal charge of minor possession... it's not something that they routinely book people for--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When he first encountered this young man, did he have any way of knowing whether the young man was, in fact, a student?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --No. Other than that he came out of Orton Hall, he appeared to be very young, appeared to be obviously under 21.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There's a great many people of that age who might come out of that hall and have no connection with the university.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Sure. He has no idea. As you remember, Mr. Overdahl suggested that he be allowed to return without the officer, and I think it would be rather naive to think that a young man disappears... now maybe this young man would have come back, but I would suspect good police procedure would lead one to be a little cautious about letting a man on a campus with 16,000 disappear in the dorm. I doubt he'd ever come back. I mean, that's just my personal reaction. If I was a law enforcement officer I wouldn't let him out of my sight.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why didn't you give that answer to me when I asked you that question?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: I thought I did, but I--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you didn't give it until you got it from the Chief Justice.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --I apologize.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I thought you gave it to him, you gave it very clearly in response to my question. Mr. Carpenter, when he got upstairs, did I understand you to say earlier that there were exigent circumstances which justified his entry into the room?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And had there not been is the implication that he could not have entered the room?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, I--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In the absence of exigent circumstances?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --I believe he could have entered on either one of the state's principal theories. Either as... you have to remember, he was only at the doorway 35 to 45 seconds.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know, but what I'm trying to get, Mr. Carpenter, do I understand your position to be that in the absence of exigent circumstances, he should not have entered the room?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: No, that is not my position. I believe there are two ways he could have entered that room. One, to accompany the arrestee, and I argue in the brief that if it is not a simultaneous entry, it's a contemporaneous entry. I think it's... 35 seconds while he's standing there and then walks in. It seems to me that the fact that he paused at the door for 30 to 45 seconds is not a great deal of constitutional significance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what are the exigent circumstances, assuming that there--

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Okay. The state could argue that the exigent circumstances in this particular case are simply that if the officer had left, the tray of marihuana seeds which he observed approximately eight feet in the room from where he was standing would have I think clearly been gone when he came back. Or it's reasonable to assume that they would have been gone. There were two people in the room; they could have eaten them, they could have hidden them in body cavities, they could have thrown them out the window, and I won't speculate what the dispersion pattern from the 11th floor of marihuana seeds if you flip them out the window. But I think it's reasonable, as the trial court and the Court of Appeals both concluded, that it was not reasonable for the officer to leave that room to get warrant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --Now, incidentally, the officer had a radio, didn't he?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it was after he entered the room that he summoned help on the radio.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why do you suppose he didn't summon held while he was at the door where he could see everything?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, he could have summoned Lieutenant Kenny--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He could see both the people, he could still see the seeds--

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --Then we'd have two officers standing at the door if he can't enter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --What was the rationale used by the court below in this decision?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Which court? The Washington Supreme Court or the Court of Appeals or the trial court?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I said the court below, the one immediately below is the Washington Supreme Court.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Oh, excuse me. Their rationale was one that we never... that there were not exigent circumstances and we didn't argue them, which is clearly erroneous because the record is replete from our trial memorandum that we argued exigent circumstances. The other was simply that New York vs. Payton and Reddy prohibited entry without a warrant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would it be your position that if the office was accompanying this young man down the hall and he was headed from room B, which was another few steps, but he walked by room A and he glanced into the room and saw the very same things that he saw in this case; namely, whatever it was on that stand.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Everything from that point forward proceeds the same?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then he enters that room, saying these things are in plain view, I can enter this room and seize them because of exigent circumstances. Is that your position?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, you have to remember in this particular case--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, just take my facts.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --Well, are the facts the same? I mean, in this case Mr. Overdahl opened the door all the way.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the door is standing open and they are both walking by, add all of a sudden the officer sees what he saw in this case sitting on the stand. Would that authorize his entry into the room.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: And he sees the occupant who responds to him?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, no occupant at all. He just sees on the stand what he sees here.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, there's a lot more here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, how about my question?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Okay, he walks by an open room and he sees the seeds.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He sees those seeds standing there... he sees what he saw in this case. He doesn't really know they were seeds. He had to go over to find out.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Assuming no one associated with the room or otherwise sees the officer and knows he has seen something, no, there wouldn't be.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's just like if you were walking by a house and looked through the window and saw them.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That's right, you need a warrant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Exactly. [Laughter]

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: But here there is an appreciable distinction. Here, he looks in that room, sees the contraband in plain view or open view I guess is the terminology we use--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, and then what?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --He sees a nervous reaction of Mr. Chrisman, he sees Mr. Chrisman moving the box around to the medicine cabinet, and then when he sees the officer in uniform gets all nervous and moves it back.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if he saw the same thing through... if he was walking by on the street, he looked in the window and he saw what he saw and he saw a man in there looking nervous and was about to destroy them, could he go in?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Assuming that... are you assuming, Justice, that--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm not assuming anything. I just tell you what he sees.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --Well, if it's apparent the man realizes the officer has seen it, I would think so, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, does the university dormitory room necessarily occupy the same status as a single family dwelling or an apartment for purposes of the Fourth Amendment?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well I'd like to say it doesn't, but I think it probably does, in the sense that it's a premises, a living quarters, like--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's his home, isn't it, for--

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --It is his home for all practical purposes. I would like to say it isn't, but I think, to be candid, sure it does.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --Mr. Smith, so long as we're into hypotheticals, suppose we take Justice White's hypothetical, and instead of seeing some seeds in this room with which he had no concern... that is, he had no concern about the room... he saw a sawed off shotgun. Do you mean to tell me that he shouldn't do anything about it?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: I'm not telling you that. I think, Chief Justice, he clearly should.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I just wanted to eliminate any idea that a policeman should ignore that. Or suppose he saw a body that appeared to be a dead body, and on reaching in and feeling the pulse, thereby violating the premises to that extent, he finds that the person is dead. He's certainly going to do something about it, isn't he?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: One would hope. I would think he should be suspended or fired if he didn't do something.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: It's correct police procedure. I don't think the Fourth Amendment requires officers to disregard things. And all the state's position is on the first issue in this case is that there were, in fact, exigent circumstances.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If they see the evidence of an unlawful, criminal act, they must do something, whatever that something is.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, with the additional that when the occupant or occupants of the premises appreciate the officer's... the word is out, so to speak, the know the thing is up, if the officer leaves I just don't think it's reasonable to expect under the Fourth Amendment that--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, your premise for this position is that he knew what he saw.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --Well, the Court of Appeals and the trial court believed that. They believe there was a reasonable basis--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you think that he did know exactly what he saw. He didn't have to go over and find out what was there.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --No, no, I think--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He may have suspected it, but did he really know.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --The respondent tries to argue, Mr. Justice, that--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Carpenter, at that point let me ask how far did you say, again, he was?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --Okay, he's 8 to 10 feed from the doorjamb to the black tray with the marihuana seeds and--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which is less than the distance between you and this bench.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --That's right. And this is an experienced officer, trained and experienced in controlled substances. There's no question in my mind from reading the record that the officer knew and believed that he had probable cause that it was marihuana.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Carpenter, my understanding is that this respondent was under arrest at the time.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Supreme Court of Washington so held.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you've been talking now for quite a while, or responding to some questions about situations where officers looking through windows and standing at doorways, as to what rights they may have. I would think you would argue this case as to the rights of an officer where he has made a lawful arrest to stay with the party arrested and to observe anything that he could observe in those circumstances.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: I think that's correct, Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Doesn't the fact that this involved a lawful arrest and a search incident to arrest distinguish this case from much of what we've been talking about?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: I think that's correct, and that's the second principal issue in this case. I think this case can be decided either on the first issue or the second issue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Carpenter, I'm confused now. I have heard you talk about exigent circumstances, I have heard you talk about search incident to a lawful arrest, and I think I've heard you talk about plain view. What is it you're really relying on? Are you relying on the fact, as you allege it to be, that the officer was in a place where he had a right to be and he saw items, contraband, in plain view? Or are you relying on exigent circumstances, or search lawful... incident to a lawful arrest, or all three or what?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Yes, Justice O'Connor, unfortunately, the last court before this Court, the Washington Supreme Court... and not to criticize that court, but I think it improperly labeled where the officer was as a nicety, and they said we're not going to decide that issue. I think it's pretty important.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what are you relying on?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Our position is... and I suppose the prosecutor wants the best of both worlds... I think we can rely on both the exigent circumstances; one, what he saw in open view while he was standing out in a public place and the occupant's reaction to him; and two, that he had a right, regardless of that, to accompany the arrestee into the premises to start with. And so, pursuant to the arrest status, for his protection, the protection of the public, the protection of the evidence, protection of veracity, so I think I'm arguing that we have the best of both worlds.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And he sees something in plain view? I'm still confused what the thrust of your argument is.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, I guess the first argument is in open view, he sees it, it's in a public place, that's exigent circumstances. Now even setting that aside, I think that... the state would like to believe that answers the issue. But setting that aside and forgetting about exigent circumstances for a moment, we also would argue that if he was, in fact, in the room, or when in the room to accompany the arrestee, once he got in the room, it's in plain view, clearly he had a right to go into the room, attendant to the arrest status of Mr. Overdahl, and that it comes under, you know, proper police procedure to accompany Mr. Overdahl in that room, and what he sees there is in plain view.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, unlike Justice O'Connor, who has recently joined our Court, do you think that any of the courts in the country or any prosecutor or any defense lawyer can be faulted for being somewhat confused about the state of Fourth Amendment law?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, I've been practicing law for ten years, and the cops always call me up at 3:00 in the morning and want me to give them a precise answer to the Fourth Amendment, and I can't do it. I think it's so enamored with constitutional subtleties and what I like to call the crafting of legal technicians that we've totally confused not only the lawyers and the judges, but the police officers who are supposed to be constitutional lawyers out in the street and making a 15-second decision to decide what the U.S. Constitution means. All I'm saying in this case is that the conduct was reasonable. The officer did what was reasonable under the status of the law clearly in 1978, in any event. That was before Mincey vs. Arizona and before Payton. It's a very confusing area. This case is important maybe to clarify what... first of all, what is an exigent circumstance. I, for the life of me, could not find a real good U.S. Supreme Court case that clearly found an exigent; I found a lot that said it wasn't and said what you should be looking for.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Carpenter, on the question of exigent circumstances, the Chief Justice gave you examples of seeing a gun or a dead body or something like that. Is it your view that the character of the crime, the degree of violence associated and so forth has anything to do with the exigent circumstances inquiry?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: No, I don't--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You would treat this case involving--

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --I don't prescribe to the theory that it has to be a serious crime--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --involving the use of marihuana as equally as serious as a murder or anything else.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --And particularly when we get into the second issue in this case about arrest, I just don't think it's reasonable to say a officer can only take reasonable precautions to protect himself when it's a serious offense. It's not uncommon for officers to be shot on a minor traffic stop. All kinds of minor things. As the amicus points out in their brief, arrest status sort of brings out the worst in some people, and in the most low key looking situation, an officer may find himself in a very precarious position.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I guess it's not uncommon to find marihuana in college dorms, either.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Oh, no, very common. It's a way of life at WSU, I believe.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Carpenter, are you familiar with a case we decided last June, I think the title of it was New York against Belton?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Yes, I am. And I think you decided Robbins or something like that the same day.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. But the Belton case, as I recall, involved an officer who stopped an automobile and made a lawful arrest and the Court held that the officer was entitled, without a warrant, to search anything in the driver's compartment of the car... passenger compartment of the car.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That's right. And I believe that case made--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Anything that he could find there. Now, the plain view doctrine certainly applies... I've always understood it... when an officer makes a lawful arrest. Why do you avoid relying primarily on that issue? Am I overlooking something?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --I excuse myself. I don't mean to... I guess like I said, I'm trying to have the best of both worlds. I think this case can be decided on both issues.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You're trying to argue three cases at one time.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, I suppose if you can find three ways to hang your hat on it, I... you know, in Washington state, the prosecutor loses 80 % of the time on all issues before the Washington Supreme Court in the last two or three years, so we try to cover the issues. We try to find some way to sustain our-- [Laughter]

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I guess you can hang your hat on which ever one you want.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: I'd like to have my convictions sustained.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, there is a difference between the Belton case and this. There we had the mobility of an automobile, which had a special character. Here you have an immobile place.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: But the language I find particular significant maybe to this case and Belton and maybe the Robinson and Gustafson cases, are the Court's observations that arrested people are very unpredictable. You know, an officer has to do enough to protect himself.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, this officer might reasonably be reprimanded for not having gone in and followed him around to be sure he wasn't going to come out to the door with a sawed off shotgun in his hand.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: I would not have done what this officer did. I would have gone in immediately. Because if you look at the record you will see that the young man gets into a drawer or a closet or something. Who knows there's not a pistol or something in there? I just think that he should have done a little more to protect himself than he did, but like I said, they're trying to act low key on the campus. They don't want to get a lot of bad publicity. That's pretty clear from the Joint Appendix.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Counsel, does the record show when this man was given his Miranda warning?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Okay, he was first given his Miranda warnings after the officer seized the tray of marihuana. At that point, he gave both the occupants their Miranda warnings, and at that juncture asked if there was anything else in the room, and at that point Mr. Chrisman went ahead and volunteered the little box the officer had seen him carrying around. It had $112 and some marihuana in it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So he wasn't given Miranda warnings--

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Not prior to the entry.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --until he was arrested for the marihuana.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He wasn't given Miranda warnings about the liquor.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And yet, the purpose of the ID was for the liquor.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, the purpose of the ID, the state would like to say is that you have to ID anybody that you arrest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where did you get that idea that you have to get an ID everytime you arrest a man?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, I believe it's correct police procedure. You have to ultimately book them under a name, and you have to verify them, and when you have... we do have a problem with people even appearing in our district court under phony names. I prosecuted a couple young men a couple months ago on just that. They went clear through the conviction stage using a phony name.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there a requirement that you identify yourself?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, I think it's reasonable for an officer to ask them; I don't know if you can force somebody to identify himself, but I think it's reasonable police procedure--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is it reasonable to take a man back to his home after you arrest him?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --If he wishes to go, whether it's for a coat or drugs or ID, you know, prescription medicine, why ever he wants to go back. I think it's the humane thing to do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you don't have a right to go there, do you?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, I think he doesn't have a right to go home, either, unless he allows you to accompany him. And if he says okay, I want to go back--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you don't have a right to accompany him unless--

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --That's right, that's our argument. It seems that if he's going to go back, then he has to have a modified right there. It's not reasonable to let him go back by himself. At least, that's the state's position.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --Mr. Carpenter, can we be certain the Washington court based its decision on the Fourth Amendment to the federal Constitution rather than on the Washington constitutional provision?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, that's one issue the respondent argues in their brief, and, of course, we argue that there's absolutely no discussion of Article I, Section 7 in the Washington state constitution in our decision. They talk about the Fourth Amendment and they talk about Payton and Rettig, and it seems to me clear that that's what they decided the case on. Now, looking at the Simpson case cited by the respondent, we argue that's really just a limited case on standing in response to this Court's case Racus. Looking at, I think it's State vs. Smith, it's in my reply brief, the Court clearly a couple years ago in Washington said that the two sections, Article I, Section 7 of the Washington constitution and the Fourth Amendment of the U.S. Constitution should be substantially interpreted the same. And so, it's the state's position that they should be, and that's what was done. I think it's mere speculation on respondent's part that it could have been. Whether it could or couldn't, it doesn't appear that they ever talked about the Washington state constitution in this case. If there are no further questions, I would like to reserve the remainder of my time for rebuttal.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Very well, Mr. Carpenter. Mr. Patrick?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. If this case is to be decided on federal constitutional grounds, I would submit to the Court that a fundamental precept of the Constitution, that possibly this case should be considered in conjunction with or determined, is a portion of the Constitution which is seldom cited and neither counsel has cited, frankly, but seldom cited because it's part of the Preamble, and that part of the Preamble says "to secure the blessings of our liberty to ourselves and our posterity. "

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the Preamble has never been considered part of the Constitution for adjudication purposes, has it?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: No, Your Honor, no, it has not. But I think that the Fourth Amendment was adopted in part to give rise or life to that portion of the Preamble; that is, "to secure the blessing of liberty. " and part of the blessing of liberty would be the right to be free from unreasonable search and seizure.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, are you suggesting that, Mr. Patrick, to negate the thought that the policeman, once having placed this man under arrest, had a right, if not a duty, to keep him within arm's length at all times until he had completed all the formalities? Any question about that in your mind?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I have to say to Your Honor that it is my judgment that the police officer probably had, as long as he had placed the individual under arrest, that the police officer could be with him as he went wherever this individual went. I do not believe, however, that the police officer had the right to accompany this... to have this individual go to his room to secure the identification. I also would submit to the Court that the police officer did not have the right to accompany him specifically into the room--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Whose idea was it to go to the room? The officer's or the arrested persons's?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --I believe it was, in essence, the officer because the officer left the individual no choice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't it ambiguous from the Supreme Court of Washington where it says at rage 35 of the Petition for Certiorari, "The officer noticed the bottle of gin and suspected that Overdahl was under the age of 21. " "He stopped Overdahl and requested his identification. " "Overdahl stated that he would have to go upstairs to his room to get the identification. " "Officer Daugherty required that he would have to accompany him to his room. " It's kind of hard to say from that description, isn't it, who... whether there was insistence on one party or the other?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Not from our standpoint, Your Honor. I submit that the insistence was for ID, and the officer wanted that ID. Not having advised Mr. Overdahl of his Miranda rights, he did not have the right to insist on ID.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was the arrest lawful, do you concede, even though he had no Miranda rights?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Your Honor, I'll be frank with you in this. I have vacillated back and forth in my own mind on it. The officer--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It makes a difference, doesn't it?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --The officer... I believe at that point, at that point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What point is that?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: The point where the police officer said, I will have to go with you to the room--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: At the elevator? Was that near the elevator or something?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --Yes. He was in the lobby. He motioned Mr. Overdahl over and he said, I'll have to see some ID for that gin, or words to that effect. Mr. Overdahl then I said I don't have it with me, I'll have to go up to my room and get it. If you'll wait for me, I'll bring it back. And the officer said no, under these circumstances I will have to go with you. And later on during cross examination--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, at what point was he arrested?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --I believe at that point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was a lawful arrest, even though there were no Miranda warnings?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: No, I do not believe at that point that the arrest was lawful, Your Honor, because the police officer at that point knew that he could not get past basically a motion to dismiss at the trial if all he came before the judge was... if all he brought before the judge... was I've arrested this individual and this individual looks like he's under 21, to me.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Wasn't he charged for just having liquor on the campus?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Wasn't one of the charges just being in possession of liquor on the campus?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: That was in violation of a Washington Administrative Code, Your Honor, but the police officer indicated that he would not have arrested that individual, in essence, for that; there would be no criminal actions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What the officer's subjective intent was I don't know is controlling. He had, in fact, committed a violation of that regulation.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Of the Washington Administrative Code apparently, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And was that not a violation for which he could have been issued a citation of some kind?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: He could have... frankly, I'm not really sure that he could be issued a citation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I'm not really sure that he could be issued a citation as such. He would certainly go to... it would go to the office of the dean of students, and there would have been initiated... I do not believe there would have been initiated any criminal sanctions for that. They would have gone to the dean of students and he may have been disciplined through whatever university disciplinary procedures.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: On page 35, the Supreme Court of Washington again says, as they were waiting for the elevator, the officer asked Overdahl how old he was. Overdahl responded that he was 19.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Yes. At that point I believe he now had the probable cause to arrest. But I believe that the custody of Mr. Overdahl--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What about Miranda warnings?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --I believe the custody of Mr. Overdahl--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No. What about when he conceded, I am 19. Was he entitled to Miranda warnings if--

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --Yes, Your Honor, he was entitled to the Miranda warnings.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --Well, do you make any point of the fact he didn't get them?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even though he conceded he was 19, without the Miranda warnings you suggest, or you argue, that the arrest was still unlawful?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. I am arguing that when the officer said, I will accompany you to the room, that he now had put... he had so restricted Mr. Overdahl's freedom of movement that he was now under arrest. And at that time he had a duty to advise Mr. Overdahl of his rights under Miranda. I believe it happens that that arrest may--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm sorry, but how can the respondent take advantage of the failure to give Miranda warnings to his roommate?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --Because it is the--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The failure to give Miranda warnings doesn't vitiate the arrest, does it?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --But that's what led directly to... as indicated in our brief, that's what led directly to the opening of the door, which was a giving up of the right of privacy to the room. And under... then that brings into play Bumper and, I believe, Crewes that--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I simply don't follow this argument. If the arrest is valid, it doesn't matter whether he gave Miranda warnings or not, does it?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --I believe that... yes, Your Honor, I believe it does because he wanted to get that ID for proof of an element of the offense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you would infer that; your opponent infers something different on that. But regardless of his motive, if he made a valid arrest, as Justice Powell has suggested, what difference does it make whether... I just don't follow your argument.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: The reason that it becomes important, Your Honor, is that the sole reason for going to that room was for identification. The identification would have--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Done two things. He could have ticketed him without taking him to the station, and he would have had written as well as oral proof of age.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Granted all that, still, now what's your argument?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I believe that at that point, not having advised him of his rights to Miranda, he did not have a right to, in essence, insist that the individual goes to his room, or even, there was no need for the individual to go to his room. And I believe that the police officer then took advantage of the situation to get the individual to his room.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I still don't follow. The failure to give Miranda warnings makes subsequent statements inadmissible in evidence. Is there any doctrine that says that failure to give Miranda warnings makes it wrong for a police officer to take a person in custody to his room?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, when going to the room is to obtain that evidence. I believe that having put the individual under arrest, having failed to advise the individual of the rights under Miranda--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And so the evidence is inadmissible. Even though it's not the person who was ultimately charged.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --Yes, the concept, from my view anyway, Your Honor, the concept is that the police officer could not have stood outside the door and beaten Mr. Overdahl to a pulp and said open the door, and Mr. Overdahl finally opens the door. I believe that Mr. Chrisman could argue and just as effectively would have standing to assert the manner in which that door was opened. Because his privacy to the room was invaded because of a violation of his co tenant's right to privacy. I believe that's in essence what Bumper ruled when the mother allowed the police officer to come in.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But what about this expression that was raised by the Chief Justice a little while back. Under all the circumstances you say he comes in the room and finds a loaded, double barreled, sawed off shotgun.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I believe, Your Honor, at that juncture there is, in fact, I believe, a difference in the quality of the item; not... at that point the officer is at least is interested in his personal safety and the personal safety of the people around as he would be evidence. And I believe that the sawed off shotgun could just as easily be confiscated under exigent circumstances--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But he wouldn't have seen if, to quote you, he hadn't violated the roommate's rights.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --That is correct, that assumes that he is properly at the door.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, doesn't that cut back on your point a little bit?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's not absolute, is it?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: No, it is not, Your Honor. I believe he would have a better case to go in to seize it for his own protection. But still and all, it was there because of a violation of constitutional rights.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, suppose it was poison there.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Poison, which can kill just as easily as a shotgun. And which can also be poured down the toilet and disposed of.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: In this case, there was no toilet in the room, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was there a window in the room?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: There was a window in the room.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It could be thrown out of the window.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Yes, if they wanted to break the window.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, they could raise the window; they weren't crippled, were they?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I believe at that point, once the window was raised--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I just don't understand how you can bootstrap these arguments. Even if you admit that you... if the prosecutor here admits that he had no right to follow the man but he did follow him, that he should have given him Miranda warnings but he didn't. I don't see how that helps your client. That's my problem.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --Your Honor,--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even if I think it helps the other guy, I don't think it helps your client. Now, does that make it clear?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --Yes, Your Honor, but as I indicated, I believe that the police officer, as far as my client, Mr. Chrisman, is concerned, if the police officer secured the opening of the door by beating up on Mr. Overdahl until he finally, through an act of submission, opened the door,--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which is not here.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --No, but the principle I think is going to be the same. A violation of a constitutional right should give the co tenant the right to object to the manner in which the privacy was given up.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean the right... looking into an open door is the same as breaking the door down.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: For constitutional--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There's a little bit of difference.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --Well, Your Honor, if you--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Patrick, I wonder if even that follows. Supposing he lawfully arrested him downstairs, and on the way up he beated him up in the elevator and violated his constitutional rights. And then said, I still am going to follow you into the room. If he has a right to follow him into the room, the fact that he beat him up in the elevator wouldn't destroy that right, would it? I mean, there would be remedies for what he did, but I'm not sure one of the remedies is that he has to turn him loose.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --I don't believe he has to turn him loose, Your Honor, but I'm not sure that he can--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if he can keep him under arrest and if you can stay with your arrestee and go into a room, it doesn't seem to me that if you did something else that's very wrong on the way in has changed the legal posture at all.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --I think that if you fail... if you violate the individual's rights and in violating those rights of that individual, the constitutional rights of that individual--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Beat him up in the elevator.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --Right, Your Honor. If that goes to the validity of the consent to go to the room--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under the theory, at least the one Justice Powell has been suggesting, he didn't need consent. He had the man under arrest, and the man goes in the room, he has a right to follow him in. And I'm just suggesting that he doesn't lose that right because he did something terrible in the elevator; if he has the right.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --You may be right, Your Honor, unless it goes to the consent of the individual to go to the room, and then I have to go back to the proposition--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But see, it you have the arrest situation, then you don't need the consent.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --Except that, Your Honor, just simply because an individual is under arrest doesn't mean that the police officer has to take him to the room.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see what you're saying. One more hypothetical. He does everything you say to one of the men, and as he gets to the door, the wind blows the door open and he sees all of that. He can't seize it. Do you want to bet?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Where the wind has blown the door open?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. Just as he got there. Fortuitous wind.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I think that's a fortuitous event all right, Your Honor, but constitutionally... well again, we go back to the proposition... I have to go back to the proposition that the police officer was there because, under our theory of the case, he had not adequately advised the individual of his rights under Miranda, and therefore--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well the police officer employed by the university sure had a right to be standing in that hallway.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --He had a right to be in the hallway, that's true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the door blew open, on my hypothetical.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I think, Your Honor, that when the doorway was blown open, it has not now come open by an event which one of the defendants was in control of. None of the parties was in control of. It's a little bit like the Chief Justice's question I think where the police officer is bringing Mr. Overdahl down the hall and the police officer sees some marihuana in another room. And in that case, I would say he could go in.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the Chief Justice says you might have to get a warrant, but you said he doesn't even have to get one.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: No, no, I'm sorry, Your Honor, I don't mean to infer that he didn't have to get a warrant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought that you just said that that would be a different case and the officer could do it. But failure to give Miranda warnings does not vitiate an otherwise lawful arrest, does it? It just means that a motion to suppress may be granted, but we'll suppress items that were surrendered by the victim under the... because of the failure to advise him of his rights. It doesn't mean that the state can't prove its case by other evidence.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: That's my understanding, Your Honor, that is correct. I do not believe that the state can prove its case by evidence that would arise from the individual.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's... but this isn't the same individual.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: But it is an individual who is in the same position, as I indicated earlier... at least I would submit to the Court that when the individual has... when a co tenant has given up the privacy of the premises because of a violation of that co tenant's rights, the non consenting co tenant should, I submit, stand in the same shoes, because it is the protection of the privacy of the premises that I believe is at stake. And this Court has on several occasions indicated that it will certainly assert and recognize the rights of the people who are... who have possessory interest in the property, although that is not necessarily the key. But again I believe that Bumper handles the standing question in which the mother, I believe it was, who owned the premises gave... acceded to the request of the police officers to come in under the proposition that they had a search warrant. And it was the defendant then who was given standing to assert that the entry was invalid because it was under the mistaken hypothesis that the police officer did have a search warrant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Tell me, Mr. Patrick, assuming there is no infirmity whatever in the arrest of Overdahl and they went up to the room, as they did, might not... or do you think the officer could accompany Overdahl into the room?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Under the facts of this case, Your Honor--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, I suggested them. If there is no infirmity whatever in the arrest of Overdahl. You go up to the room because Overdahl has said, I want to get the ID for you. Could not the officer accompany him into the room?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --The Washington Supreme Court said no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What do you think?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Ah--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And why not, if you're going to answer he couldn't? I would suppose the officer would be able to be sure that he wasn't going to be attacked or assaulted.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --That's why I said I didn't think so under the facts of this case. I can understand why a police officer would want to be in a position where he could protect himself from an attack or an assault. The problem is, in this case, a police officer's actions did not give any indication that he had any concern about that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because he stood in the doorway instead of entering, is that it?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: He stood in the doorway, he had not frisked the individual before he got there, at least the record doesn't disclose that he did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let's suppose, however, that he had entered. Would there be anything wrongful about that entry?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: If he had gone into the room?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. If he had gone in with Overdahl.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Under our Supreme Court's decision, which we submit could be supported under the Washington constitution, and under State vs. Seiss, a New Jersey decision we cited in our brief, the theory is that there are some types of arrests which are so insignificant that it is not valid for the police officer to accompany the individual into the room.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Incidentally, there is no suggestion that the Washington Supreme Court decided this case under the Washington constitution, is there?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: The Washington Supreme Court cited State vs. Daugherty, which is a Washington case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, do you think they rested this on the state constitution rather than the federal?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I think that they could have and I'm not 100 % sure.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, did they?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I'm not really sure, frankly, whether they did or did not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If they did, we would have no jurisdiction over this case, obviously.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, do you suggest they did?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Yes, I'm suggesting that this Court... if this Court believes that the search could be upheld under federal constitutional grounds, I believe that the case should be remanded back to the State Supreme Court to see if the State Supreme Court decided the case under the state constitution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why did the State Supreme Court cite all the federal constitutional cases if it was deciding it under the Washington constitution?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I believe, Your Honor, that the concept that the states can interpret their own constitution and can grant additional rights over and above those granted to the federal Constitution frankly is of relatively recent origin, and I don't think it's been thoroughly thought out, frankly. I believe in Mr. Justice Brennan's Law Review article he propounded that proposition. And I think that frankly, part of the reason would be, although it's up to the state supreme court to say for themselves, but I would think that part of the reason is that the state supreme courts have grown so accustomed to examining questions in light of what they think the United States Supreme Court is going to rule, that they are very used to taking precedent from the U.S. Supreme Court. And I believe, however, that in the future, as this theory is... we all have more experience with this theory, it would not surprise me to see state supreme court decisions resting their decision on their state constitutional grounds where it is their view that additional rights are granted under their respective constitutions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But we're reviewing a judgment here that's not in the future but was rendered in the past.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. But one of the authorities cited by the state supreme court was, as I say, the State vs. Daugherty. Now, State vs. Daugherty itself cited some federal cases and some state cases, and I did review some of those state cases and again, those cases cited are also state and federal cases. I believe that the state supreme court could have decided this case under the state constitution, and as I indicated earlier, if this Court feels that the state constitution... or rather, that the federal Constitution was not violated by the search, then I would request the Court, again, to remand it back to the state supreme court for a determination as to whether or not the search could be sustained under the state constitution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't the test whether it did decide it rather than whether it could have decided it, under Privda and Minnesota vs. National Tea and those cases?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Your Honor, I think that may well be, but I think that where the supreme court, or where the case... if the case were to be reversed and go back to the trial court, I would think that if the trial court then were to sentence the individual and impose a sentence, that again, we would probably be faced with trying to go back up to the state supreme court and give the state supreme court an opportunity to rule on the constitutionality of... under the state constitution. And I believe it would avoid a lot of circular action if it were simply to go back to the state supreme court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of course, this business of deciding cases on state constitutional principles rather than federal is nothing new. We've been doing it up here for a long time.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, that is true. But I think in the... I believe certainly in recent history under the state court... under the constitutional principles, the state supreme courts have... particularly after Mann... have become accustomed to following the precedent of the U.S. Supreme Court, and feel... I believe for a time felt pretty much locked in by the precedents set by this Court. And the state supreme court then should be allowed, I submit, to decide this case on state constitutional principles, if the Court determines that this case should not be decided as incorrect under federal constitutional principles.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Patrick, on that point, did you tell us... I want to be sure I understood what you were saying... that there is a state constitutional doctrine that more or less classifies certain offenses as very petty offenses and that if this offense is in that category, that as a matter of state law, the entry into the room might not have been justified by the fact that the man was under arrest.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: That's my... the state supreme court said that this type... an arrest for this type of offense did not justify the police officer accompanying the individual into the room.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's in the... is that the Daugherty case the courts have found that--

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: No, that's my understanding of the Chrisman case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --Of the holding in this case, I see, all right.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: Yes. The Simpson case, which we cited for the proposition that the state court can interpret its own constitution and indeed has interpreted in some instances the state constitution to give the additional rights than those given under the federal Constitution, we submit was not simply standing, because standing is not a constitutional interpretation, as I see it. So I believe the state constitution could vindicate the holding of the state court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Patrick, may I review with you just a moment to be clear, are you now willing to concede that Mr. Overdahl had been lawfully arrested before the officer accompanied him to his room?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: I believe that when... yes, Your Honor, when the police officer found out that he was under the age of 19, I believe that at that point--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The answer is yes?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And do you concede that the officer was lawfully at the doorway of the room with Mr. Overdahl?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: No, Your Honor, because I do not believe that the police officer had given Mr. Overdahl his Miranda rights, and therefore, did not have the right to accompany him to get evidence against Mr. Overdahl. And that if anything--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you would concede there are circumstances which would justify an officer in accompanying the person arrested to his dwelling, into the dwelling.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --I believe the case authority is that the police officers, on certain occasions where there... and all the cases cited by the state indicate that the request was for a purpose of the accused rather than a purpose of the police officer, where that is permitted. I believe that... I haven't touched yet on the knock and announce rule, and I would like to just touch very briefly on the knock and announce rule. If the police officer were outside the room and had accompanied Mr. Overdahl to the room and were at that point outside the room, now the nature of the situation when he determines to enter the room, it seems to me, has been changed. He has now... he was at the room for one purpose and is outside that room and has now decided for whatever reason to go into the room, and I submit at that point, under the knock and announce rule, he should... while he might not have had to knock; I don't think he would have had to knock, but I do believe that he should have announced his purpose to enter the room.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Ker in California held that the knock and announce rule was not a constitutional rule, didn't it?

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: It was my understanding that it was of constitutional proportion, or at least it's my understanding that the state--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's what the dissent said in Ker and California.

turns.name: ROBERT F. PATRICK
turns.text: --I believe that our state supreme court has determined that it is of constitutional proportions. In summation, if I could simply say to the Court that in this case, if the police officer were outside the room, and if the Court finds exigent circumstances, he created his own exigent circumstances. He could have maintained the status quo and did not. He could have maintained with his radio a guard at the door while a search warrant was obtained, and we submit that where we are talking about protection to the dwelling, the protection in maintaining the right to privacy, that where a reasonable opportunity exists to obtain a search warrant and preserve the status quo, that this Court will require that the status quo be maintained and that a search warrant be obtained and that the entry be made pursuant to a search warrant. Thank you, Your Honors.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Do you have anything further, Mr. Carpenter?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think that opinion can be read as resting on your statement?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --Which, the Chrisman opinion?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: No, I believe it cannot. And I particularly believe it cannot because of State v. Smith.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think it's even ambiguous--

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: No, I don't think there's anything ambiguous. It's true that it did cite a state case, State v. Daugherty, which cites a whole series of other state cases, but those state cases are talking about interpretation of the U.S. Constitution primarily; they're not talking about the U.S. Constitution. The only case of recent times that is helpful to underline any disparity in the two constitutions is State v. Simpson which talks about the standing problem. State v. Smith decided in 1977 clearly makes the point that the two constitutional provisions should be given the same effect. And it's only that point of departure on standing that our court made a difference. I think the record is helpful in some of the things we've been talking about. I'd ask the Court when it has a chance to look at the Joint Appendix Page 35, and I think that clearly shows the trial court's findings of fact, which show that it was Mr. Overdahl's desire to go back to the room, and that his response to the office was okay, he could accompany him. Also, a fact that got maybe lost in the wash a little bit here is that the third violation, also a criminal violation, was that... and it's replete in the Joint Appendix references... is that it's a violation of criminal law and Washington law to have an open container out in public, and that's another reason the officer could have arrested him, and one of the reasons he cites at the suppression hearing in the trial court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --An open container of gin?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Of alcoholic beverage. The gin--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In this case, gin.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: --Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: However old you are.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: However old you are, if you're 65 it doesn't matter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Carpenter, what about-- --That isn't old. [Laughter]

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: I didn't imply it was old.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What about Mr. Patrick's suggestion that there are certain very minor offenses with respect to which when an arrest is made, the arresting officer could not follow the man into the room?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: No, I don't subscribe to that theory and I don't think our court said that. I think our court just felt that the U.S. Constitution required... I think they may have thought in their principal opinion that the U.S. Constitution made some distinction between serious and non serious cases. I think that is sort of ludicrous because the officer is threatened, his protection is just as much at stake.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, in the opinions last spring, Justice Stewart referred to custodial arrests. Do you remember that in one of the cases?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Yes, I remember.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think that was kind of nonsensical?

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: Well, let me just say it this way in the most diplomatic way I can, I think that it is unrealistic to say that officers have to articulate some specific threat to them. Because by the time they find out about the threat, they are probably either Going to be dead or severely injured. Maybe that's a very callous look at society, but we have some bizarre things going on in today's society. It's not the peaceful society of 200 years ago.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So your view would be all arrests are considered--

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: All arrests. There's jeopardy, as the amicus points out in their brief, in all arrests.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --Until you get on bail.

turns.name: RONALD R. CARPENTER
turns.text: That's correct. The other thing is I really don't think this Miranda issue, the question at the elevator... I would suggest is somewhat of a red herring. You have to remember that they were already going back to the room to get ID when he asked him about his age and he said it was 19. Now, what effect does the fact that he may have violated his Miranda rights at that point? They were still going back to the room, that's where they started, that's where they were headed when they went into the elevator and ascended to the 11th floor. So it doesn't add or subtract anything. He was never prosecuted, the state never attempted to use that statement against him for any criminal prosecution. I think it just doesn't naturally follow that that somehow affects the arrest in this case. As the Court of Appeals aptly observed on page 24 through 26 of our Petition for Certiorari, the section where the Court of Appeals talked about that, and they say it's just of no constitutional significance. I see my time is up. Thank you.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Thank you, gentlemen, the case is submitted.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The honorable court is now adjourned until tomorrow at ten o'clock [= 10:00].
